title: Control Structures
subtitle: Make Decisions, Repeat Actions, Control the Flow!
overview: Every program needs to make decisions and repeat actions — that's what control structures are for. In this session, students learn how to use if/elif/else to branch their code based on conditions, and for/while loops to repeat blocks of code efficiently. By mastering control structures, students move from writing simple scripts to building programs that think, decide, and act dynamically.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — flowchart diagrams, whiteboard decision trees, student code screenshots, activity photos.

keyTopics: Control Structure — Code that controls the ORDER and CONDITIONS under which other code runs
if Statement — Runs a block of code ONLY IF a condition is True
else Statement — Runs a block of code when the if condition is False (the fallback path)
elif Statement — Checks another condition if the previous if/elif was False (short for 'else if')
Comparison Operators — == (equal), != (not equal), > (greater), < (less), >= (greater or equal), <= (less or equal)
Logical Operators — and (both must be True), or (at least one True), not (flips True to False)
for Loop — Repeats a block of code for each item in a sequence (list, range, string)
range() — Generates a sequence of numbers: range(5) → 0,1,2,3,4 | range(1,6) → 1,2,3,4,5
while Loop — Repeats a block of code AS LONG AS a condition remains True
Infinite Loop — A while loop whose condition never becomes False — the program gets stuck! (use Ctrl+C to stop)
break — Immediately exits a loop even if the condition is still True
continue — Skips the rest of the current loop iteration and jumps to the next one
Nested Loop — A loop placed inside another loop, useful for grids, tables, and patterns
Indentation — Python uses spaces (4) to define which code belongs inside an if/loop block — CRITICAL!

activity.title: Human Robot + Control Structure Coding Challenges
parts: Open floor space for the Human Robot activity
Printed challenge cards (one per student or pair)
steps: Part 1 — Human Robot: Teacher gives verbal if/else and loop instructions. Students physically follow them: 'If you are wearing blue, take 2 steps forward, else clap twice' and 'Repeat 5 times: jump once'. Discuss how this maps to code
Part 2 — if/elif/else Practice: Write a grade classifier — input a score (0-100), print 'A' (90+), 'B' (75-89), 'C' (60-74), 'D' (45-59), or 'Fail' (below 45)
Part 3 — for Loop Practice: Use a for loop with range() to print the 7 times table. Then loop over a list of student names and print 'Hello, [name]!'
Part 4 — while Loop Practice: Write a number guessing game — generate a random number 1-10, use a while loop to keep asking the user to guess until they get it right
Part 5 — break and continue: Loop through numbers 1-20, use continue to skip even numbers, use break to stop if the number exceeds 15
Part 6 — Nested Loop Pattern: Use two nested for loops to print a 5x5 star pattern (* * * * *) on the screen

Challenge: Combine everything — write a simple ATM simulator that uses a while loop for the menu, if/elif for menu choices (Check Balance, Deposit, Withdraw, Exit), and validates that withdrawal doesn't exceed balance

quiz.q: What is a Control Structure in programming?
options: A special type of variable
Code that controls the order and conditions under which other code runs
A Python library for building menus
A type of error that stops the program
quiz.explanation: Control structures direct the FLOW of a program! Instead of running every line top to bottom, they let code branch (if/else) or repeat (loops) based on conditions — making programs smart and dynamic.

options: Repeats a block of code a set number of times
Runs a block of code ONLY IF the given condition is True
Defines a new function
Imports a library
quiz.explanation: `if` is a decision maker! `if score >= 90: print('A')` — only prints 'A' IF score is 90 or more. If the condition is False, Python skips the indented block entirely.

options: Always, regardless of the if condition
Only when the if condition is True
When the if condition is False
Only when there is an error
quiz.explanation: `else` is the fallback! If the `if` condition is False, Python runs the `else` block instead. It's the 'otherwise' path — like 'If it rains, take umbrella, ELSE wear sunglasses.'

quiz.q: What does `elif` mean, and when is it used?
options: It means 'end loop if' and exits a loop
It means 'else if' and checks another condition when the previous if/elif was False
It means 'equal if' and compares two values
It is another name for the else statement
quiz.explanation: `elif` = else if! It lets you check multiple conditions in order. Python checks each condition top to bottom and runs the FIRST block whose condition is True, skipping all the rest.

quiz.q:
What is the output of this code?
`x = 15`
`if x > 20:`
`    print('Big')`
`elif x > 10:`
`    print('Medium')`
`else:`
`    print('Small')`
options: Big
Medium
Small
Big Medium
quiz.explanation: x = 15. Is 15 > 20? No. Is 15 > 10? YES! So Python prints 'Medium' and skips the else. Once a condition is True, Python runs that block and ignores all remaining elif/else blocks.

quiz.q: What do Comparison Operators like ==, >, < do in Python?
options: They assign values to variables
They perform maths operations like addition and subtraction
They compare two values and return True or False
They import modules
quiz.explanation: Comparison operators produce a Boolean result! `5 > 3` → True, `10 == 20` → False, `7 != 7` → False. They are the building blocks of all if/elif conditions.

quiz.q: What does `and` do in a condition like `if age >= 13 and age <= 17`?
options: The condition is True if EITHER part is True
The condition is True only if BOTH parts are True
It adds the two ages together
It checks if age is between 13 and 17 separately
quiz.explanation: `and` requires BOTH conditions to be True. `age >= 13 and age <= 17` is True only if age is at least 13 AND no more than 17 — so ages 13, 14, 15, 16, 17 pass. 12 or 18 would fail.

quiz.q: What does `for i in range(5):` do?
options: Repeats the loop body 5 times, with i taking values 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
Repeats the loop body 5 times, with i taking values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
Checks if i is less than 5
Creates a list of 5 random numbers
quiz.explanation: `range(5)` generates 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — five numbers starting from 0! So the loop runs 5 times. If you want 1 to 5, use `range(1, 6)`. Remember: range() always starts at 0 unless told otherwise.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between a `for` loop and a `while` loop?
options: for loops are faster than while loops
for loops repeat for each item in a known sequence; while loops repeat as long as a condition is True
while loops can only count numbers; for loops can iterate over lists
There is no difference — they do exactly the same thing
quiz.explanation: for = known repetitions (loop over this list / run exactly N times). while = condition-based repetitions (keep going UNTIL this becomes False). Use for when you know the count; use while when you don't!

quiz.q: What is an Infinite Loop and why is it dangerous?
options: A loop that runs exactly once
A loop that skips every other iteration
A while loop whose condition never becomes False, causing the program to run forever and freeze
A for loop that goes backwards
quiz.explanation: Infinite loop = stuck program! If `while True:` has no break, or the condition never changes, the loop never stops. The program freezes and uses 100% CPU. Always make sure your while loop condition can eventually become False!

quiz.q: What does the `break` statement do inside a loop?
options: Pauses the loop for 1 second
Skips the current iteration and moves to the next one
Immediately exits the loop, even if the condition is still True
Restarts the loop from the beginning
quiz.explanation: `break` is an emergency exit! The moment Python hits `break`, it jumps OUT of the loop completely — no more iterations. Very useful in while loops to exit when a goal is reached (e.g. correct password entered).

quiz.q: What does the `continue` statement do inside a loop?
options: Exits the loop immediately
Skips the REST of the current iteration and jumps to the next one
Continues to the next program after the loop
Adds one more iteration to the loop
quiz.explanation: `continue` skips the rest of the CURRENT iteration only, then the loop carries on. Example: looping 1–10 with `if i % 2 == 0: continue` skips even numbers but keeps looping — printing only odd numbers.

options: A loop that runs inside a conditional if statement
A loop placed inside another loop
A loop that repeats fewer times than expected
Two loops that run at the same time
quiz.explanation: Nested loops = loop inside a loop! For every ONE iteration of the outer loop, the inner loop completes ALL its iterations. Great for printing grids, tables, and patterns. A 5×5 star grid needs two loops of range(5).

quiz.q: Why is INDENTATION critically important in Python control structures?
options: It makes the code look prettier
Python uses indentation (4 spaces) to define which lines of code belong inside an if block or loop — wrong indentation causes errors or wrong behaviour
It helps the compiler run faster
It is optional — Python ignores spaces
quiz.explanation: Python uses INDENTATION instead of curly braces {}! The 4 spaces before a line tell Python it belongs inside the if/loop above it. Missing or wrong indentation causes IndentationError or makes code run in the wrong place — a very common beginner mistake!

quiz.q: A student wants to print numbers 1 to 10 but skip number 7. Which combination should they use?
options: for loop with break when i == 7
for loop with range(1, 11) and continue when i == 7
while loop with i starting at 7
An if/else statement without any loop
quiz.explanation: `for i in range(1, 11):` loops 1 to 10. Add `if i == 7: continue` to skip 7 — continue jumps to the next number without printing 7. break would stop the whole loop at 7, printing only 1–6.
